questions.prompt: Green organisms that make their own food using sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide.
questions.explanation: Producers (autotrophs) are organisms like plants and algae that build sugars from sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide through photosynthesis. They form the base of every food chain and supply the chemical energy that animals later eat. Without producers, an ecosystem collapses because no other organism can capture sunlight directly. The 19th-century scientist Jan Ingenhousz showed plants release oxygen in light. NYS Grade 5 Science Standard 5-LS1-1 expects students to support an argument that plants get materials for growth chiefly from air and water, not soil.

questions.prompt: Meat-eating animals like hawks and wolves that hunt other animals for food.
questions.explanation: Carnivores are consumers that get energy by eating other animals. Secondary carnivores eat herbivores (a hawk eating a rabbit), and tertiary carnivores eat other carnivores (an orca eating a seal). Each step loses about 90% of the available energy as heat, which is why food chains rarely have more than four or five links. Long Island red-tailed hawks and great white sharks are local examples. 5-LS2-1 expects students to trace this energy flow from sun to producers to consumers in a model.

questions.prompt: The green pigment in plant leaves that captures sunlight for photosynthesis.
questions.explanation: Chlorophyll is the green-colored molecule inside plant chloroplasts that absorbs red and blue wavelengths of sunlight and reflects green light, which is why leaves look green. Russian botanist Mikhail Tsvet first separated chlorophyll types using chromatography in 1900. When chlorophyll breaks down in autumn, hidden orange and yellow pigments show through, producing Long Island's fall foliage. NYS 5-LS1-1 and 5-PS3-1 expect students to know that the Sun's energy enters the food chain through this pigment in plant leaves.

questions.prompt: A diagram showing how energy and matter move through many connected food chains in an ecosystem.
questions.explanation: A food web is a model that links many food chains so students can see how a single producer feeds several herbivores, each of which feeds several carnivores. Energy flows in one direction from Sun to producers to consumers, but matter cycles when decomposers return nutrients to the soil. Charles Elton built the modern food-web concept in his 1927 book Animal Ecology. NYS 5-LS2-1 expects students to develop a food-web model to describe how matter cycles and energy flows, which is a top assessment task on the Grade 5 elementary science test.

questions.prompt: A diagram that shows how the amount of energy gets smaller at each higher level of a food chain.
questions.explanation: An energy pyramid is a stacked-bar diagram showing that only about 10% of the energy at each level of a food chain transfers to the next level, with 90% lost as heat. The producer base is widest and top predators are at the narrow tip. American ecologist Raymond Lindeman published the modern energy pyramid in 1942. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS3-1 expects students to model how energy in food originally came from the Sun, and energy pyramids appear regularly on the state elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The colorless gas plants take in from the air to make food.
questions.explanation: Plants pull carbon dioxide (CO2) from the surrounding air through tiny leaf openings called stomata, then use it as the carbon source for sugar during photosynthesis. NYS 5-LS1-1 specifically asks students to support the claim that plants get materials they need for growth chiefly from air and water, not from soil. Belgian researcher Jan Baptista van Helmont's 1640s willow-tree experiment showed a plant gained 164 pounds while the soil lost only 2 ounces, which became one of the first pieces of evidence for this idea.

questions.prompt: The process plants use to turn sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into sugar and oxygen.
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis is the chemical process where chloroplasts use light energy to combine carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen. Jan Ingenhousz showed in 1779 that only green parts of plants in sunlight produce oxygen, and Joseph Priestley showed in 1771 that plants 'restore' air burned by candles. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS1-1 expects students to explain that plants build their bodies from air and water, and 5-PS3-1 expects them to describe that the energy in food originally came from the Sun through this process.

questions.prompt: Stored energy in food that an animal can use later, originally from sunlight.
questions.explanation: Chemical energy is energy stored in the bonds of molecules like sugars, fats, and proteins. Plants store sunlight as chemical energy in glucose, and animals release that energy through respiration to power movement, growth, and heat. Antoine Lavoisier showed in the 1780s that breathing and burning both release the same kind of energy from food and fuel. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS3-1 expects students to describe that the energy in food comes originally from the Sun and was stored as chemical energy by green plants.

questions.prompt: When two organisms need the same limited resource and only one can have it.
questions.explanation: Competition happens when organisms need the same limited resource, like food, water, sunlight, mates, or shelter, and the resource is too scarce for all of them. Plants in a forest compete for sunlight by growing tall; coyotes and red foxes on Long Island compete for the same rabbits. Charles Darwin used competition as a key driver of natural selection in 1859. NYS Grade 5 Standard 3-LS4-3 (reviewed in Grade 5) expects students to describe how competition for limited resources shapes which organisms survive in a habitat.

questions.prompt: The form of sugar plants make and store as their main food.
questions.explanation: Glucose (C6H12O6) is the simple sugar that photosynthesis produces. Plants either use glucose right away for energy through cellular respiration, or store it as starch in roots, fruits, and seeds. When a Grade 5 student eats a Long Island potato or apple, they are eating stored solar energy that a plant trapped through photosynthesis. NYS 5-PS3-1 asks students to use models to describe that the energy in animals' food was once energy from the Sun, captured by producers as glucose.

questions.prompt: All the living and nonliving parts of an area working together, like a pond or forest.
questions.explanation: An ecosystem is the full set of living organisms (biotic factors like plants, animals, bacteria) and nonliving conditions (abiotic factors like sunlight, water, soil, and temperature) interacting in a specific area. Arthur Tansley coined the term in 1935. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS2-1 expects students to develop a model that describes how matter cycles and energy flows in an ecosystem. Long Island salt marshes, the Pine Barrens, and Long Island Sound itself are local ecosystems Grade 5 students can name.

questions.prompt: The repeating one-way movement of energy through an ecosystem from the Sun.
questions.explanation: Energy flow is the one-direction transfer of energy from the Sun to producers, then to herbivores, then to carnivores, then to decomposers, with about 90% lost as heat at each step. Unlike matter, energy is never recycled, it must keep arriving from the Sun. American ecologist Howard T. Odum mapped this flow with energy pyramids in the 1950s. NYS 5-PS3-1 and 5-LS2-1 expect Grade 5 students to model this flow, especially showing how the Sun's energy becomes the chemical energy in food.

questions.prompt: The recycling movement of substances like water, carbon, and nitrogen through ecosystems.
questions.explanation: Matter cycles describe how the same atoms of water, carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen are used over and over by living things. Plants, animals, and decomposers move these atoms between the air, soil, water, and bodies. Unlike energy, which flows one way, matter is recycled forever. French chemist Jean-Baptiste Boussingault discovered the nitrogen cycle in 1838. NYS 5-LS2-1 expects students to develop a model showing the cycling of matter and the one-way flow of energy among the living and nonliving parts of an ecosystem.

questions.prompt: Bacteria and fungi that break down dead organisms and return nutrients to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers like mushrooms, mold, and soil bacteria break apart dead plants, dead animals, and waste, releasing nitrogen, phosphorus, and carbon back into the soil and air for new producers to use. Without decomposers, dead matter would pile up and nutrients would be locked away from living things. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first observed these microbes through his 1670s microscopes. NYS 5-LS2-1 makes decomposers the closing arrow that recycles matter inside an ecosystem model, while energy keeps flowing one-way from the Sun.

questions.prompt: A close, long-term relationship between two species, like clownfish and sea anemones.
questions.explanation: Symbiosis is a long-term biological relationship between two species, including mutualism (both benefit, like bees and flowers), commensalism (one benefits, the other unaffected), and parasitism (one benefits, the other harmed). German botanist Anton de Bary coined the term in 1879. Long Island examples include lichen on Pine Barrens trees (fungi + algae mutualism) and ticks on white-tailed deer (parasitism). NYS Grade 5 Science assessments use this concept to extend ecosystem models in 5-LS2-1, especially in food-web extension questions.

questions.prompt: The place where a living thing finds food, water, shelter, and space to live.
questions.explanation: A habitat is the specific natural place where an organism lives and gets everything it needs: food, water, shelter, sunlight, and space. A pine warbler's habitat is the Long Island Pine Barrens, while a horseshoe crab's habitat is the shallow sandy bottom of the Long Island Sound. Joseph Grinnell developed the modern habitat concept in 1917. NYS Grade 5 Science Standard 3-LS4-3 (covered in Grade 5 review on the state assessment) expects students to explain how an organism's habitat must supply its needs, or it cannot survive there.

questions.prompt: All the members of one kind of organism living in one area, like all the white-tailed deer in a forest.
questions.explanation: A population is every individual of one species in a specific area at one time, like the population of horseshoe crabs in Jamaica Bay or the population of monarch butterflies in a Long Island meadow. Population size changes with births, deaths, immigration, and emigration. Thomas Malthus wrote about how populations grow toward limits in 1798. NYS 5-LS2-1 expects students to model how matter and energy connect populations of producers, consumers, and decomposers inside an ecosystem.

questions.prompt: All the different populations of organisms living and interacting in one area together.
questions.explanation: A community is every living thing in an area: all the populations of plants, animals, fungi, and bacteria interacting together. In a Long Island salt marsh, the community includes cordgrass, fiddler crabs, ribbed mussels, mummichog fish, snowy egrets, and salt-marsh bacteria. The community plus the nonliving environment (water, soil, air) equals the ecosystem. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS2-1 expects students to model how all members of a community exchange matter and energy, especially through food webs and decomposition.

questions.prompt: The gas plants release into the air as a waste product of photosynthesis, which humans need to breathe.
questions.explanation: Oxygen (O2) is released as a byproduct when plants split water during photosynthesis. Joseph Priestley demonstrated in 1771 that a sprig of mint inside a sealed jar could 'refresh' the air enough for a mouse to live and a candle to burn, the first evidence that plants release oxygen. Today, about 21% of Earth's atmosphere is oxygen, with most coming from ocean phytoplankton and land plants. NYS Grade 5 Science Standard 5-LS1-1 and 5-PS3-1 ask students to connect plant photosynthesis to the breathable air around them.

questions.prompt: Removing matter or energy from an ecosystem faster than it can be replaced, often hurting living things.
questions.explanation: Human impact refers to ways people change ecosystems by clearing forests, polluting water, overfishing, building roads, or releasing carbon dioxide that warms the climate. Long Island examples include nitrogen runoff from lawns causing harmful algae blooms in the Sound. American ecologist Rachel Carson's 1962 book Silent Spring linked pesticides like DDT to falling osprey populations. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science to protect Earth's resources.

questions.prompt: The star that supplies almost all the energy used by living things on Earth.
questions.explanation: The Sun is the medium-sized star at the center of our solar system whose nuclear fusion of hydrogen into helium supplies the light and heat that powers nearly every process on Earth, including photosynthesis, weather, and the water cycle. The Sun is about 93 million miles from Earth and about 4.6 billion years old. Hans Bethe explained solar fusion in 1939. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS3-1 expects students to use a model to describe that energy in animals' food comes from the Sun and 5-ESS1-1 asks why the Sun looks brighter than other stars.

questions.prompt: Animals that eat only plants for their energy, like deer or rabbits.
questions.explanation: Herbivores are primary consumers that get all of their energy by eating producers such as grasses, leaves, or fruit. In a Long Island salt marsh, marsh rabbits and grasshoppers feed directly on cordgrass, transferring chemical energy upward. Herbivores have flat grinding teeth and long digestive tracts adapted to break down tough cellulose. NYS Grade 5 Science Standard 5-LS2-1 asks students to develop a model of how matter and energy move among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment, and herbivores are the second arrow in that model.

questions.prompt: The unit of measurement scientists use to describe energy stored in food.
questions.explanation: A Calorie (capital C) on a food label is actually 1,000 calories of energy, or one kilocalorie, and measures how much heat the food can release when burned. French scientist Nicolas Clément introduced the calorie as a heat unit in 1824. A Grade 5 student needs about 1,800 to 2,200 Calories per day. NYS 5-PS3-1 builds toward this idea by asking students to trace energy from the Sun through producers and into animal food, and Grade 5 assessment items often include food-label data tables.

questions.prompt: The continuous movement of water between Earth's surface, the air, and the ground.
questions.explanation: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water between oceans, lakes, the atmosphere, and the land through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and infiltration. Solar energy drives evaporation, while gravity pulls precipitation back down. About 97% of Earth's water sits in salty oceans and only 3% is fresh. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 asks students to develop a model of Earth's major systems and 5-ESS2-2 asks them to describe and graph the amounts of salt water and fresh water in different reservoirs.

questions.prompt: The process where animals release energy from food using oxygen.
questions.explanation: Cellular respiration is the chemical process inside body cells that combines glucose with oxygen to release energy, carbon dioxide, and water. It is essentially the reverse of photosynthesis. Antoine Lavoisier first showed in 1789 that animals consume oxygen and release CO2 like a slow fire. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS1-1 and 5-PS3-1 expect students to trace how plants build sugar from air and water, then animals release that energy through respiration, completing the matter-and-energy story.

questions.prompt: The process where liquid water from oceans and lakes turns into water vapor and rises into the air.
questions.explanation: Evaporation happens when liquid water absorbs heat energy from the Sun and changes into invisible water vapor, rising into the atmosphere. About 90% of the water vapor in the air comes from ocean evaporation, and 10% from plant transpiration. Italian physicist Evangelista Torricelli first measured atmospheric pressure in 1643, leading to today's water-cycle science. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model how Earth's geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere interact, with evaporation as the first arrow of the water cycle.

questions.prompt: When water vapor cools and turns back into tiny droplets, forming clouds in the sky.
questions.explanation: Condensation occurs when water vapor cools in the upper atmosphere, slows down, and clusters around tiny dust particles to form liquid droplets that we see as clouds, fog, or dew. The temperature at which water condenses is called the dew point. John Aitken first explained cloud formation around dust nuclei in 1880. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model the water cycle, including how condensation links evaporation to precipitation in Earth's atmosphere.

questions.prompt: The layer of air, gases, and clouds that surrounds Earth.
questions.explanation: The atmosphere is the layer of gases surrounding Earth, made of about 78% nitrogen, 21% oxygen, 0.04% carbon dioxide, and small amounts of argon and water vapor. It has five layers: troposphere, stratosphere, mesosphere, thermosphere, and exosphere. Weather happens in the troposphere, where Grade 5 students live. Léon Teisserenc de Bort discovered the stratosphere in 1902. NYS 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model how the atmosphere interacts with the other Earth systems through processes like the water cycle and weather.

questions.prompt: Water falling from clouds as rain, snow, sleet, or hail.
questions.explanation: Precipitation is any form of water that falls from clouds, including rain, snow, sleet, hail, and freezing rain. It happens when cloud droplets grow large and heavy enough that gravity overcomes air resistance. Long Island averages about 45 inches of precipitation per year, mostly rain. Norwegian meteorologist Tor Bergeron explained the modern rain-forming process in 1933. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to use a model of the water cycle to describe how water moves between Earth's atmosphere, land, and oceans.

questions.prompt: Water from rain or melting snow that flows over land into streams, rivers, and oceans.
questions.explanation: Runoff is rain or snowmelt that flows downhill across the surface, eventually reaching streams, rivers, and oceans. It carries soil, fertilizer, and pollutants from the land, which is why fertilizer runoff into Long Island's Great South Bay can cause harmful algae blooms. About 30% of all precipitation becomes runoff. Robert Horton developed runoff equations in 1933. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 includes runoff as part of the water cycle model and 5-ESS3-1 expects students to think about how communities can protect water resources from runoff pollution.

questions.prompt: The condition of the atmosphere in a place at a specific time, like today's temperature and rain.
questions.explanation: Weather is the day-to-day state of the atmosphere, including temperature, humidity, wind, precipitation, clouds, and air pressure. It changes quickly. Meteorology, the study of weather, became a scientific field after Galileo's thermometer in 1592. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model Earth's atmosphere as one of its major systems, and Grade 5 students must distinguish weather (a day's conditions) from climate (long-term patterns), a frequent question on the NYS elementary science test.

questions.prompt: Anything harmful added to air, water, or land that hurts living things.
questions.explanation: Pollution is the release of harmful substances into the air, water, or soil. Common types include air pollution from burning fossil fuels, water pollution from fertilizer and oil, and plastic pollution in oceans. Long Island faces nitrogen pollution from septic systems entering its bays. Rachel Carson's 1962 book Silent Spring warned about pesticide pollution. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 directly asks students to obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science to protect Earth's resources from pollution and environmental harm.

questions.prompt: The long-term average weather pattern of a region over many years.
questions.explanation: Climate is the long-term average of weather conditions in an area over 30 or more years, including typical temperature, rainfall, wind, and seasons. Long Island has a humid continental climate with cool winters and warm humid summers. Russian climatologist Wladimir Köppen built the modern climate-classification system in 1884. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 asks students to model Earth's systems including the atmosphere, and 5-ESS2-2 asks them to compare amounts of fresh and salt water, which connects to regional climate.

questions.prompt: Using natural resources carefully so they last for the future, like turning off lights or saving water.
questions.explanation: Conservation means protecting and carefully using natural resources so they last for future generations. Examples include turning off lights, fixing leaky faucets, planting trees, and protecting wildlife habitat. President Theodore Roosevelt, a New Yorker, championed conservation and set aside 230 million acres of public land between 1901 and 1909. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to combine information about ways individual communities use science ideas to protect Earth's resources, environments, and biodiversity through conservation.

questions.prompt: The long-term warming of Earth caused largely by humans adding extra carbon dioxide to the air.
questions.explanation: Climate change is the long-term warming of Earth's climate caused largely by burning fossil fuels that release greenhouse gases like carbon dioxide and methane. Atmospheric CO2 has risen from 280 ppm in 1750 to over 420 ppm by 2024. Swedish scientist Svante Arrhenius first calculated the warming effect of CO2 in 1896. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to obtain and combine information about ways individual communities use science to protect Earth's resources, including responding to climate change.

questions.prompt: All the water on Earth: oceans, rivers, lakes, ice, and underground water.
questions.explanation: The hydrosphere is the system of all of Earth's water in all its forms: oceans, rivers, lakes, glaciers, groundwater, and water vapor. About 71% of Earth's surface is water, and 97% of that is salty ocean. Eduard Suess introduced the modern hydrosphere concept in the 1880s. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to develop a model using examples of how Earth's geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere interact. 5-ESS2-2 expects them to compare and graph the amount of water in each Earth reservoir.

questions.prompt: A storm with strong rotating winds that forms over warm ocean water, like Hurricane Sandy.
questions.explanation: A hurricane is a giant rotating tropical storm with sustained winds of 74 mph or more, fueled by warm ocean water. Hurricane Sandy struck Long Island in October 2012, killing 53 New Yorkers and causing $19 billion in damage. Hurricanes form near the equator and spin counterclockwise in the Northern Hemisphere because of the Coriolis effect, first described by Gaspard-Gustave de Coriolis in 1835. NYS 5-ESS3-1 expects students to combine information about ways communities use science to protect Earth's resources and people from severe storms.

questions.prompt: All the solid rocks, soil, and landforms that make up Earth's surface and interior.
questions.explanation: The geosphere is everything solid in Earth, including the crust, mantle, outer core, and inner core. It includes rocks, soil, sand, mountains, and the seafloor. The crust under Long Island is part of the North American plate. Eduard Suess used 'geosphere' in his 1880s book The Face of the Earth. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model the interactions of Earth's four spheres, with the geosphere supplying nutrients to plants, supporting structures, and providing minerals for the biosphere and hydrosphere.

questions.prompt: Water that soaks into the ground and is stored between soil and rock layers underground.
questions.explanation: Groundwater is freshwater that has soaked into the ground and filled the spaces between soil grains, sand, and rocks. It is stored in underground reservoirs called aquifers and provides drinking water for almost all of Long Island through wells in the Upper Glacial, Magothy, and Lloyd aquifers. About 30% of Earth's freshwater is groundwater. Henry Darcy developed the equation for groundwater flow in 1856. NYS 5-ESS2-1 and 5-ESS2-2 expect students to model and quantify how water moves through Earth's systems, including underground.

questions.prompt: The exchange of matter and energy among Earth's air, water, rocks, and living things.
questions.explanation: Earth system interactions describe how Earth's four spheres (atmosphere, hydrosphere, geosphere, biosphere) constantly exchange matter and energy. The water cycle, the carbon cycle, weathering, and photosynthesis all link multiple spheres. American oceanographer Roger Revelle co-founded modern Earth-system science in the 1950s. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 directly asks students to develop a model using examples to describe ways the geosphere, biosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere interact, which is a top-tier assessment task.

questions.prompt: The salty water that covers about 71 percent of Earth's surface.
questions.explanation: Ocean water makes up about 97% of all the water on Earth and covers 71% of the planet's surface. It contains about 3.5% dissolved salts (mostly sodium chloride). Long Island sits between the Long Island Sound and the Atlantic Ocean. Marine biologist Sylvia Earle has explored more than 7,000 hours underwater since the 1960s. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-2 specifically asks students to describe and graph the amounts and percentages of water and fresh water in various reservoirs to provide evidence about the distribution of water on Earth.

questions.prompt: Water with very low salt content, like in lakes, rivers, and underground aquifers.
questions.explanation: Fresh water is water with less than 1% dissolved salts and includes water in glaciers, lakes, rivers, ponds, swamps, and groundwater. Only about 3% of Earth's water is fresh, and most of that is locked in glaciers and ice caps. Long Island's drinking water comes from fresh groundwater in three aquifers. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-2 directly asks students to describe and graph the relative amount of fresh water in different reservoirs to show how little fresh water Earth has.

questions.prompt: An underground rock or sand layer that holds large amounts of freshwater.
questions.explanation: An aquifer is a porous underground layer of rock, sand, or gravel that holds and lets water flow through it. Long Island sits on three major aquifers (Upper Glacial, Magothy, and Lloyd) that supply almost all 3 million residents with drinking water. Henry Darcy developed the math of aquifer flow in 1856. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-2 expects students to describe the distribution of fresh water on Earth, and 5-ESS3-1 asks how communities use science to protect water resources like aquifers from pollution and overuse.

questions.prompt: Large frozen freshwater stores at the North and South Poles and on tall mountains.
questions.explanation: Glaciers and ice caps are massive sheets of frozen freshwater, holding about 69% of Earth's fresh water. The Antarctic ice sheet alone stores enough water to raise sea level by 60 meters if it all melted. Louis Agassiz proved an Ice Age once covered much of Long Island in 1837, leaving behind the moraines that form Long Island's hilly North Shore. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-2 expects students to graph the small percentage of accessible fresh water versus the much larger amount locked in glaciers.

questions.prompt: The amount of dissolved salt in water, measured in parts per thousand.
questions.explanation: Salinity is the measure of dissolved salts in water, usually expressed in parts per thousand (ppt). Average ocean salinity is about 35 ppt, while fresh water has less than 1 ppt. Brackish water in places like the mouth of Long Island's Peconic River has middle values. Robert Boyle first measured ocean salinity in 1670. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-2 expects students to describe fresh versus salt water, and salinity data tables often appear on the state Grade 5 science assessment.

questions.prompt: Reusing materials like paper, glass, and aluminum to keep them out of landfills.
questions.explanation: Recycling is the process of collecting and reprocessing used materials (paper, glass, aluminum, plastic, steel) into new products instead of throwing them in landfills. The U.S. recycles about 32% of municipal waste. Recycling one aluminum can saves enough energy to run a TV for 3 hours. The modern recycling symbol was designed by Gary Anderson in 1970. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to combine information about ways communities protect Earth's resources, and recycling is a top student-action answer on the state Grade 5 science assessment.

questions.prompt: Energy sources that will never run out, like sunlight, wind, and moving water.
questions.explanation: Renewable energy comes from sources that are constantly replaced by nature, including solar, wind, hydroelectric, geothermal, and biomass. New York's South Fork Wind farm off Long Island became the state's first offshore wind farm in 2024. American engineer Charles Fritts built the first solar cell in 1883. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to obtain and combine information about ways communities use science ideas to protect Earth's resources, and switching to renewables is a top assessment answer.

questions.prompt: A long period with much less rain than normal, harming crops and water supplies.
questions.explanation: A drought is an extended period (months to years) of below-average rainfall that causes water shortages, dry soil, crop failure, and wildfires. Long Island experienced severe droughts in 1965 and 2002 that lowered groundwater levels in Suffolk County. The U.S. Drought Monitor maps drought severity weekly. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS3-1 expects students to obtain and combine information about ways communities use science to protect Earth's resources from problems like drought, and assessment items often include drought-impact data tables.

questions.prompt: All the living things on Earth, from bacteria to plants to humans.
questions.explanation: The biosphere is the global sum of every living thing on Earth, including bacteria, fungi, plants, and animals, plus the parts of the atmosphere, hydrosphere, and geosphere where life exists. Russian scientist Vladimir Vernadsky developed the modern biosphere concept in 1926. Life on Earth has existed for about 3.5 billion years. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS2-1 expects students to model how the biosphere interacts with Earth's air, water, and rocks, and 5-LS2-1 connects this to ecosystem-level matter cycles and energy flow.

questions.prompt: The protective layer of gases high in the atmosphere that blocks harmful UV rays from the Sun.
questions.explanation: The ozone layer is a region of Earth's stratosphere about 9 to 22 miles up that contains a high concentration of ozone (O3) molecules. It absorbs about 97% of the Sun's harmful ultraviolet (UV) radiation, protecting life from skin cancer and crop damage. American scientists Mario Molina and Sherwood Rowland discovered in 1974 that CFCs from spray cans were destroying ozone, leading to the 1987 Montreal Protocol. NYS 5-ESS3-1 connects directly to human-action stories like the ozone-hole recovery.

questions.prompt: Earth's natural satellite that orbits Earth about once every 27 days.
questions.explanation: The Moon is Earth's only natural satellite, about 238,855 miles away, with a diameter about one-fourth of Earth's. The Moon orbits Earth once every 27.3 days (sidereal month). Apollo 11 astronauts Neil Armstrong and Buzz Aldrin first walked on the Moon on July 20, 1969. The Moon has no atmosphere and temperatures swing from 250°F in sunlight to -250°F in shadow. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of daily changes in shadows, seasons, and the appearance of the Moon.

questions.prompt: The time of day when our part of Earth faces the Sun and we have light.
questions.explanation: Day is the period when a location on Earth faces the Sun because of Earth's rotation, lasting about 12 hours on average but longer in summer and shorter in winter. Long Island gets about 15 hours of daylight on the June solstice and 9 hours on the December solstice. Eratosthenes measured Earth's tilt and circumference around 240 BCE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data of daily changes in the length of shadows and time of sunrise/sunset across seasons.

questions.prompt: Earth's yearly trip around the Sun, taking about 365 days.
questions.explanation: Revolution is Earth's yearly trip around the Sun, taking 365.25 days, which produces the year and the seasons (along with Earth's 23.5° axis tilt). Nicolaus Copernicus proposed in 1543 that Earth revolves around the Sun, replacing the Earth-centered model. Earth travels about 584 million miles in one orbit at 67,000 mph. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data of seasonal patterns of sunrise and sunset positions, which depend on Earth's tilted revolution.

questions.prompt: When Earth's shadow falls on the Moon and the Moon looks dark or red.
questions.explanation: A lunar eclipse happens when Earth passes between the Sun and the Moon, casting Earth's shadow on the Moon. During a total lunar eclipse, the Moon often glows red because Earth's atmosphere bends red sunlight onto the Moon's surface, which is why it is sometimes called a 'blood moon.' Christopher Columbus famously used a predicted lunar eclipse in 1504 to convince native Jamaicans to feed his crew. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 builds toward eclipse patterns through daily and seasonal Moon-position data.

questions.prompt: The different visible shapes of the Moon during its monthly orbit, like crescent, full, and new.
questions.explanation: Moon phases are the different shapes of the lit-up part of the Moon we see from Earth as the Moon orbits us every 29.5 days (synodic month). The eight phases are new, waxing crescent, first quarter, waxing gibbous, full, waning gibbous, last quarter, and waning crescent. Half the Moon is always lit by the Sun, but we see different amounts of that lit half. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to graph daily changes in Moon appearance to reveal the monthly phase pattern.

questions.prompt: The downward pull that keeps you on the ground and makes objects fall.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the invisible pulling force every object with mass exerts on every other object. On Earth, gravity pulls things toward Earth's center at about 9.8 meters per second squared. Sir Isaac Newton formulated the law of universal gravitation in 1687, allegedly after watching an apple fall. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 expects students to support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down toward the planet's center, a top-frequency Grade 5 assessment item.

questions.prompt: The direction Earth's gravity pulls every object, from up high to the planet's center.
questions.explanation: Earth's gravity always pulls objects toward the planet's center, which we experience as 'down' no matter where we stand on Earth's surface. A person in Long Island and a person in Australia are both pulled toward the same center, but their 'down' directions are opposite from a space view. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 directly asks students to support an argument with evidence that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down, which is the highest-confidence Grade 5 force-and-motion item on the state assessment.

questions.prompt: The amount of matter in an object, measured in grams or kilograms.
questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured in grams or kilograms. Mass stays the same no matter where the object is, unlike weight, which depends on gravity. A 1-kilogram brick has the same mass on Earth, the Moon, or in space, but it weighs about 6 times less on the Moon because lunar gravity is weaker. Antoine Lavoisier emphasized mass conservation in chemistry in 1789. NYS Grade 5 connects this to gravity (5-PS2-1) and to matter properties (5-PS1-1).

questions.prompt: The measurement of how strongly gravity pulls on an object's mass, measured in pounds or newtons.
questions.explanation: Weight is the force of gravity acting on an object's mass, measured in newtons (N) or pounds. Weight changes with location: a Grade 5 student weighing 80 pounds on Earth would weigh about 13 pounds on the Moon and about 30 pounds on Mars because those bodies have weaker gravity. Sir Isaac Newton defined weight as mass times gravitational acceleration in 1687. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 builds toward weight by establishing that gravity always pulls objects toward Earth's center.

questions.prompt: The largest object in our solar system, whose strong gravity holds the planets in orbit.
questions.explanation: The Sun has 99.86% of all the mass in our solar system, and its gravitational pull holds Earth, the seven other planets, and dwarf planets in their orbits. The Sun is about 864,000 miles wide, 109 times Earth's diameter, and 333,000 times Earth's mass. Johannes Kepler's three laws of planetary motion (1609-1619) and Newton's law of gravitation (1687) explain why planets orbit the Sun. NYS 5-ESS1-1 expects students to argue that the Sun appears brighter than other stars because it is closer.

questions.prompt: A huge ball of glowing hot gas in space that produces its own light.
questions.explanation: A star is a giant ball of glowing plasma (mostly hydrogen and helium) held together by gravity, with nuclear fusion in its core producing energy that escapes as heat and light. Our Sun is one star out of more than 100 billion in the Milky Way galaxy. American astronomer Cecilia Payne-Gaposchkin discovered in 1925 that stars are mostly hydrogen. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 expects students to support an argument that the apparent brightness of the Sun and stars is due to their relative distances from Earth.

questions.prompt: Earth's home planet, the third one from the Sun.
questions.explanation: Earth is the third planet from the Sun, about 93 million miles away, with a diameter of 7,917 miles. It is the only known planet with liquid surface water and life. Earth orbits the Sun once every 365.25 days and rotates once every 24 hours. Nicolaus Copernicus established Earth as a planet orbiting the Sun in 1543. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 expects students to think about Earth's relationship to other stars, and 5-ESS2-1 expects them to model Earth's four interacting spheres.

questions.prompt: The reason the Sun looks much brighter than other stars in the sky.
questions.explanation: The Sun looks much brighter than other stars because it is much closer to Earth. The Sun is only 93 million miles away, while the next closest star, Proxima Centauri, is more than 25 trillion miles away. If you placed the Sun where Proxima Centauri is, the Sun would look like just another point of light. Friedrich Bessel made the first reliable star-distance measurement in 1838. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 directly asks students to support an argument that the Sun's apparent brightness comes from its relative closeness.

questions.prompt: A pattern of stars in the sky that people group together and name, like Orion or the Big Dipper.
questions.explanation: A constellation is a group of stars that form a recognizable pattern in the night sky, named for animals, objects, or mythological figures. Modern astronomers recognize 88 official constellations covering the entire sky, defined by the International Astronomical Union in 1930. Greek astronomer Ptolemy listed 48 constellations in his 'Almagest' around 150 CE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns of seasonal star positions, which is why some constellations are summer-only and others winter-only.

questions.prompt: The giant collection of about 200 billion stars that includes our Sun, shaped like a flat spiral.
questions.explanation: The Milky Way is the spiral galaxy that contains our Sun and at least 100-400 billion other stars. It is about 100,000 light-years across, and our Sun sits about 27,000 light-years from the center. On a dark Long Island night, the Milky Way appears as a hazy band of light. American astronomer Edwin Hubble proved in 1924 that other galaxies exist beyond our own. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 expects students to think about stars and their distances, which sets up later galaxy lessons.

questions.prompt: Earth's daily spin on its axis, which causes day and night.
questions.explanation: Rotation is the spinning of Earth on its axis, completing one full turn every 24 hours, which produces day and night. Earth rotates west-to-east at about 1,000 mph at the equator. Léon Foucault's 1851 pendulum experiment in Paris proved Earth rotates. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data showing daily and seasonal patterns of shadows and apparent Sun motion, all caused by Earth's rotation. Grade 5 students should distinguish rotation (daily, day/night) from revolution (yearly, seasons).

questions.prompt: When our part of Earth turns away from the Sun and we see darkness.
questions.explanation: Night happens when a location on Earth rotates away from the Sun, so sunlight cannot reach it. Earth's 24-hour rotation produces alternating day and night. Long Island has about 9 hours of night in summer and 15 hours in winter because of Earth's axis tilt of 23.5 degrees. Léon Foucault's 1851 pendulum proved Earth rotates. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to graph the daily change in shadow lengths and the seasonal pattern of when the Sun rises and sets, all caused by rotation and tilt.

questions.prompt: The dark shape an object makes on the ground when it blocks the Sun's light.
questions.explanation: A shadow is the dark area created when an opaque object blocks light. The length and direction of a shadow change throughout the day as the Sun moves across the sky and across the seasons as the Sun's angle changes. Shadows are longest at sunrise and sunset and shortest at noon. Eratosthenes used noon shadows to calculate Earth's circumference around 240 BCE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 directly asks students to represent data of patterns of daily and seasonal changes in shadow lengths and positions.

questions.prompt: The four yearly periods (spring, summer, fall, winter) caused by Earth's tilt and orbit.
questions.explanation: Seasons are the four yearly weather periods (spring, summer, fall, winter) caused by Earth's 23.5° axis tilt as it orbits the Sun. When Long Island tilts toward the Sun in June, we get summer with long days and high Sun angles. When we tilt away in December, we get winter with short days. Hipparchus identified the seasonal cycle in 120 BCE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data of seasonal patterns of sunrise and sunset, length of day, and shadow length, all explained by tilted revolution.

questions.prompt: The two days each year (around June 21 and December 21) with the longest and shortest amounts of daylight.
questions.explanation: A solstice is the day each year when the Sun reaches its highest or lowest point in the sky, producing the longest day (summer solstice around June 21) or the shortest day (winter solstice around December 21). At Long Island's latitude (about 41°N), the summer solstice has about 15 hours of daylight. Ancient peoples marked solstices at Stonehenge (3000 BCE) and Newgrange. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 builds toward solstice ideas through seasonal sunrise/sunset and shadow-length data.

questions.prompt: The biggest planet in the solar system, a gas giant with a Great Red Spot.
questions.explanation: Jupiter is the largest planet in our solar system, with a diameter of 86,881 miles, more than 11 times Earth's. It is a gas giant made mostly of hydrogen and helium, with a famous Great Red Spot storm over 300 years old. Galileo Galilei discovered Jupiter's four largest moons (Io, Europa, Ganymede, Callisto) on January 7, 1610, providing key evidence for the Sun-centered model. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 and 5-PS2-1 build understanding of how the Sun's gravity holds planets like Jupiter in orbit.

questions.prompt: Small rocky bodies that orbit the Sun, mostly between Mars and Jupiter.
questions.explanation: Asteroids are small rocky and metallic bodies orbiting the Sun, most found in the Asteroid Belt between Mars and Jupiter. The largest, Ceres, is about 583 miles wide and is now classified as a dwarf planet. Italian astronomer Giuseppe Piazzi discovered Ceres in 1801. Asteroids are leftovers from the solar system's formation 4.6 billion years ago, and a 6-mile-wide asteroid strike likely killed the dinosaurs 66 million years ago. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 builds toward this through the bigger solar-system distance picture.

questions.prompt: The Sun, planets, moons, asteroids, and comets all held by the Sun's gravity.
questions.explanation: The solar system is the Sun plus everything held by its gravity: 8 planets, dozens of moons, dwarf planets like Pluto, asteroid belts, the Kuiper Belt, and the Oort Cloud. The solar system formed about 4.6 billion years ago. Nicolaus Copernicus established the Sun-centered model in 1543, and Galileo's 1610 telescope observations of Jupiter's moons confirmed it. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 builds the foundation by asking students to argue that the Sun's brightness from Earth depends on distance, and 5-ESS1-2 extends to seasonal patterns.

questions.prompt: Icy objects that develop a glowing tail when they get close to the Sun.
questions.explanation: Comets are icy bodies (sometimes called 'dirty snowballs') that orbit the Sun, mostly far out in the Kuiper Belt and Oort Cloud. When a comet gets close to the Sun, the ice vaporizes and forms a glowing coma and a tail that can stretch millions of miles. Halley's Comet, identified by Edmond Halley in 1705, returns every 76 years (next in 2061). NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 builds the foundation for understanding all the objects in the solar system held by the Sun's gravity.

questions.prompt: A small rocky icy world like Pluto that is too small to be a full planet but bigger than an asteroid.
questions.explanation: A dwarf planet is an object that orbits the Sun, has enough gravity to be round, but has not cleared its orbital neighborhood of other objects. The International Astronomical Union created the category in 2006, reclassifying Pluto. The five recognized dwarf planets are Ceres, Pluto, Haumea, Makemake, and Eris. American astronomer Mike Brown's 2005 discovery of Eris triggered Pluto's demotion. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 builds toward understanding the solar system as a system of many objects held by the Sun's gravity.

questions.prompt: Matter with a definite shape and a definite volume, like a rock or ice cube.
questions.explanation: A solid is a state of matter with definite shape and definite volume. The particles in a solid are packed tightly in fixed positions and only vibrate in place. Examples include rock, ice, wood, and metal. When ice melts at 32°F (0°C), the solid water becomes liquid. Robert Hooke first described the particle nature of solids in 1665. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 expects students to develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen, which works for all three states.

questions.prompt: The amount of matter in an object, measured with a balance in grams or kilograms.
questions.explanation: Mass is the amount of matter in an object, measured using a balance in grams (g) or kilograms (kg). Mass is conserved during changes of state, mixing, and chemical reactions: matter is never created or destroyed. Antoine Lavoisier established conservation of mass in 1789, calling it 'nothing is lost, nothing is created.' NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 expects students to measure and graph mass to support the idea that the total mass is conserved regardless of physical or chemical changes.

questions.prompt: A change like melting, freezing, or boiling, where no new substance is made.
questions.explanation: A physical change is a change in shape, size, or state of matter where no new substance is made. Examples include melting ice, dissolving sugar, tearing paper, and boiling water. The original substance can usually be recovered (cooled water re-freezes into ice). NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 expects students to measure and graph that mass is conserved during physical or chemical changes, and 5-PS1-4 expects them to tell physical changes (like dissolving) apart from chemical changes (like reacting) by spotting whether a new substance forms.

questions.prompt: Matter that has no definite shape or volume and spreads out to fill its container, like air or steam.
questions.explanation: A gas is a state of matter with no definite shape and no definite volume, expanding to fill its container. Gas particles move freely and far apart at high speeds. Examples include the oxygen and nitrogen in air, helium in balloons, and steam from boiling water. Italian scientist Amedeo Avogadro proposed in 1811 that equal volumes of gases hold equal numbers of particles. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 directly addresses gases: students can prove gas has mass by weighing a balloon before and after inflating it.

questions.prompt: The change of state where a solid turns into a liquid, like ice melting.
questions.explanation: Melting is the change from solid to liquid that happens when a solid absorbs enough heat for particles to break free from their fixed positions. Each pure substance has a specific melting point: water melts at 32°F (0°C), iron at 2,800°F. Daniel Fahrenheit set 32°F as freezing/melting in 1724, while Anders Celsius set 0°C in 1742. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 expects students to measure and graph mass to support that the total amount of matter is conserved during changes like melting.

questions.prompt: The tiny pieces too small to see that make up all matter.
questions.explanation: Particles are the tiny units (atoms and molecules) that make up all matter and are too small to see with the unaided eye. Even a grain of sand contains trillions of atoms. Greek philosopher Democritus first proposed around 400 BCE that matter is made of atomos (indivisible particles). John Dalton confirmed atoms scientifically in 1808. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 directly asks students to develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen, the foundation idea of the entire unit.

questions.prompt: Matter with a definite volume but takes the shape of its container, like water or juice.
questions.explanation: A liquid is a state of matter with definite volume but no definite shape; it takes the shape of its container. Liquid particles are still close together but can slide past each other, so liquids can flow. Examples include water, milk, and oil. When liquid water freezes at 32°F (0°C) it becomes ice; when it boils at 212°F (100°C) it becomes steam. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 expects students to model that all matter, including liquids, is made of tiny particles too small to be seen with the unaided eye.

questions.prompt: The amount of space an object takes up, measured in milliliters or cubic centimeters.
questions.explanation: Volume is the amount of three-dimensional space an object takes up, measured in milliliters (mL), liters (L), or cubic centimeters (cm³). For liquids, scientists use a graduated cylinder; for solids, they use length × width × height for rectangles, or water displacement for irregular shapes. Archimedes discovered water-displacement volume around 250 BCE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to make observations and measurements of properties like volume and density to identify materials based on their properties.

questions.prompt: The change from a liquid to a gas at the surface of water from sunlight or warmth.
questions.explanation: Evaporation is the change from liquid to gas that happens at a liquid's surface as faster-moving particles escape into the air. It can happen at any temperature, while boiling happens at a specific boiling point (water boils at 212°F or 100°C). Evaporation drives the water cycle that supplies Long Island's rain and groundwater. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 and 5-PS1-2 expect students to model particles in liquids and gases and measure that mass is conserved when liquid water evaporates into a sealed container.

questions.prompt: Separating a mixture by passing it through a screen or paper that traps the solids.
questions.explanation: Filtration is a physical separation method that uses a porous material (paper, screen, or sand) to trap larger particles while letting smaller ones (and liquids) pass through. Long Island's water-treatment plants filter sand and gravel before sending drinking water to homes. Robert Hooke described early laboratory filtration in 1665. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 expects students to investigate whether mixing substances forms new substances, and separating a mixture by filtration shows that the original substances were not changed, confirming it is a mixture.

questions.prompt: Two or more substances physically combined that can be separated, like a salad or trail mix.
questions.explanation: A mixture is two or more substances physically combined without chemically bonding, so each substance keeps its own properties and the mixture can be separated by physical means like filtering, settling, or magnetism. Examples include trail mix, sand and gravel, and salad. Robert Boyle distinguished mixtures from compounds in his 1661 book The Sceptical Chymist. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 expects students to conduct investigations to determine whether mixing two or more substances results in new substances.

questions.prompt: A type of mixture where one substance dissolves completely in another, like sugar water.
questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture where one substance (the solute) dissolves completely and evenly in another (the solvent), creating a uniform mixture you cannot separate by filtering. Examples include sugar water, salt water, and air. Salty Long Island Sound water is a solution of sodium chloride in water. Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius developed the modern theory of solutions in 1884. NYS 5-PS1-4 expects students to investigate whether mixing produces new substances, and dissolving sugar in water keeps it a mixture, not a new substance.

questions.prompt: The substance that gets dissolved in a solution, like sugar in sugar water.
questions.explanation: A solute is the substance that gets dissolved in a solution, usually present in smaller amount than the solvent. In salt water, salt is the solute and water is the solvent. The amount of solute that dissolves depends on temperature: hot water dissolves more sugar than cold water. American chemist Wilhelm Ostwald shared the 1909 Nobel Prize for solution chemistry. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to identify materials based on properties, and dissolving behavior is one such identifying property.

questions.prompt: How easily a material can be bent or shaped without breaking, like a paper clip.
questions.explanation: Flexibility is the property describing how easily a material bends, stretches, or reshapes without breaking. Metals like copper and aluminum are very flexible (malleable and ductile), while glass and most ceramics are not. Engineers test flexibility before choosing materials for bridges, springs, and Long Island Rail Road tracks. American materials scientist John Bardeen contributed to understanding metal flexibility at the atomic level (Nobel Prize 1956). NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to use flexibility as one identifying property of materials.

questions.prompt: The substance that does the dissolving in a solution, usually water.
questions.explanation: A solvent is the substance that does the dissolving in a solution, usually present in greater amount. Water is the most common solvent on Earth and is called the 'universal solvent' because it dissolves more substances than any other liquid. About 60% of an adult human body is water acting as a biological solvent. American scientist Henry Cavendish proved water is a compound (not an element) in 1781. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 builds toward solutions by establishing that matter is made of particles, including solvent molecules.

questions.prompt: A change like burning, rusting, or cooking, where a new substance is made.
questions.explanation: A chemical change is a process where one or more substances combine, separate, or rearrange to form one or more new substances with different properties. Burning wood, rusting iron, baking bread, and digesting food are all chemical changes. The original substances cannot be easily recovered. Antoine Lavoisier classified chemical reactions in the 1780s. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 directly expects students to conduct an investigation to determine whether mixing substances produces new substances (chemical change) or just a mixture.

questions.prompt: Visible bubbles forming during a reaction, often showing a new gas was made.
questions.explanation: Bubbles forming during a reaction means a new gas is being produced, which is one of the four classic clues of a chemical change. When you mix baking soda and vinegar, carbon dioxide gas bubbles up. When iron rusts, no gas appears, but a new solid forms. American chemist Joseph Priestley first isolated several new gases in the 1770s, including oxygen. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 expects students to design tests to spot chemical changes through clues like bubbling, color change, light, or temperature change.

questions.prompt: When iron metal slowly turns reddish-brown after exposure to air and water.
questions.explanation: Rusting is the chemical reaction between iron, oxygen, and water that produces iron oxide (Fe2O3), the reddish-brown flaky substance we call rust. It is a chemical change because rust has totally different properties from iron. Long Island's salty coastal air speeds up rusting on cars and bridges. English scientist Humphry Davy explained metal corrosion in 1824. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 expects students to investigate that a chemical change produces a new substance, and rust on a nail is a top-frequency assessment example.

questions.prompt: The scientific law that says matter is not created or destroyed during physical or chemical changes.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass is the scientific law stating that the total mass of all materials in a closed system stays the same before and after physical changes (like melting) or chemical changes (like burning). French chemist Antoine Lavoisier proved this in 1789 by carefully weighing sealed flasks before and after reactions. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 directly asks students to measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change, the total weight of matter is conserved, the unit's biggest idea.

questions.prompt: When wood or fuel reacts with oxygen quickly, giving off light, heat, and smoke.
questions.explanation: Burning, also called combustion, is a fast chemical reaction between a fuel and oxygen that releases heat, light, and new substances like carbon dioxide and water vapor. Burning a log of wood produces ash, CO2, and water that all together weigh the same as the wood plus the oxygen used. Antoine Lavoisier proved in the 1780s that burning is a reaction with oxygen, replacing the old phlogiston theory. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 and 5-PS1-4 use combustion as the classic test of mass conservation and new-substance formation.

questions.prompt: How heavy something feels for its size, used to identify materials like wood or lead.
questions.explanation: Density is one of the most reliable properties used to identify a substance because every pure substance has its own density. Water is 1.00 g/mL, ice is 0.92 g/mL (floats), aluminum is 2.70 g/cm³, and lead is 11.34 g/cm³. Archimedes used density around 250 BCE to detect a fake gold crown. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 directly asks students to make observations and measurements of materials to identify materials based on properties like density, hardness, color, flexibility, and conductivity.

questions.prompt: How well a material lets heat or electricity flow through it, like a copper wire.
questions.explanation: Conductivity is the property describing how well a material lets heat or electricity move through it. Metals like copper, silver, and aluminum are good conductors; rubber, plastic, glass, and wood are insulators. Long Island's power lines use aluminum and copper because they conduct electricity well. Italian physicist Alessandro Volta built the first battery in 1800 by stacking metals of different conductivities. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to identify materials by properties, and conductivity is the top property for separating metals from non-metals.

questions.prompt: The property that compares how heavy something is compared to how much space it takes up.
questions.explanation: Density is mass divided by volume (g/mL or g/cm³). Each pure substance has a unique density: water is 1.00 g/mL, ice is 0.92 g/mL (which is why ice floats), iron is 7.87 g/cm³. Greek scientist Archimedes is famous for using density to detect a fake gold crown around 250 BCE. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to make observations and measurements to identify materials based on properties, with density being one of the most reliable identifying properties on the Grade 5 assessment.

questions.prompt: How a material reacts when touched by a magnet, like iron sticking but plastic not.
questions.explanation: Magnetism is the property of attracting iron, nickel, or cobalt. A material is magnetic if it sticks to a magnet (like iron nails) and nonmagnetic if it does not (like aluminum or copper). Long Island's location near the magnetic North Pole means compass needles point slightly off true north. Pierre Curie discovered that magnetism disappears above a critical temperature in 1895. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to make observations to identify materials based on properties, and magnetism is one of the easiest classroom test properties.

questions.prompt: The temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid, like 32 degrees Fahrenheit for ice.
questions.explanation: Melting point is the specific temperature at which a solid changes to a liquid; it is a reliable property of any pure substance. Water melts at 32°F (0°C), aluminum at 1,221°F (660°C), iron at 2,800°F (1,538°C). Daniel Fahrenheit calibrated his thermometer in 1724 by using water's melting and boiling points. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-3 expects students to identify materials based on properties, and melting point is one of the most precise identifying properties of substances on the Grade 5 elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The pulling force between any two objects with mass, especially noticed when objects fall.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the invisible attractive force every object with mass exerts on every other object. On Earth's surface, gravity pulls things toward the planet's center at about 9.8 m/s² (32 feet per second squared). Sir Isaac Newton published his universal law of gravitation in 1687, after watching an apple fall. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 expects students to support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down toward the planet's center, the unit's anchor concept.

questions.prompt: The direction Earth's gravity pulls every object, toward the planet's middle.
questions.explanation: Earth's gravity always pulls toward the planet's center, which is why a person standing in Long Island and a person standing in Australia are both pulled toward the same center, even though their 'down' directions look opposite from space. The force keeps oceans on the planet and the Moon orbiting. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 directly asks students to support an argument with evidence that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down, and this is one of the highest-frequency items on the state Grade 5 assessment.

questions.prompt: The thrown rocks, dropped balls, and falling apples that Earth's gravity pulls down.
questions.explanation: Falling objects are pulled toward Earth's center at 9.8 m/s² regardless of their mass: a feather and a bowling ball fall at the same rate when air resistance is removed, as Apollo 15 astronaut David Scott demonstrated on the Moon in 1971. Galileo proposed this idea in the 1590s, allegedly by dropping balls from the Leaning Tower of Pisa. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 expects students to use evidence like falling objects to argue that gravity pulls them toward Earth's center.

questions.prompt: The slow rolling, bouncing path of a ball thrown across a field, curving back down to the ground.
questions.explanation: Projectile motion is the curved path of any thrown or launched object as it moves forward and falls due to gravity at the same time. A football, a baseball, or a thrown rock all follow a parabolic (arch) shape because of gravity. Galileo described projectile motion mathematically in 1638. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 expects students to support an argument that Earth's gravity always pulls objects down, and projectile motion is a top example of gravity's downward influence even while the object moves sideways.

questions.prompt: What you would weigh on the Moon compared to your weight on Earth, since the Moon has weaker gravity.
questions.explanation: On the Moon, you would weigh about one-sixth (1/6) what you weigh on Earth because the Moon has only one-sixth of Earth's surface gravity (about 1.6 m/s² vs. 9.8 m/s²). A Grade 5 student weighing 80 pounds on Earth weighs about 13 pounds on the Moon. Apollo astronauts could leap 6 times higher than on Earth. Mass stays the same; only weight changes. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 establishes Earth's downward gravity, and Moon-weight comparison is a frequent extension item on the Grade 5 state science test.

questions.prompt: A push or pull that can start, stop, or change the motion of an object.
questions.explanation: A force is any push or pull that can change an object's motion: starting it, stopping it, speeding it up, slowing it down, or changing direction. Forces are measured in newtons (N). Examples include the force of gravity pulling you down, the force of your hand pushing a door open, and friction slowing a sliding book. Sir Isaac Newton built the first complete theory of forces in 1687. NYS Grade 3 force-and-motion standards reviewed in Grade 5 expect students to identify forces and predict how they change motion.

questions.prompt: Two forces that are equal and opposite, so the object does not change its motion.
questions.explanation: Balanced forces are equal-strength forces pulling or pushing in opposite directions, so they cancel out and the object's motion does not change. A book resting on a table has balanced forces: gravity pulls down, the table pushes up with equal strength. A tug-of-war that goes nowhere has balanced forces. Newton's First Law (1687) explains balanced forces. NYS Grade 5 reviewing Grade 3 standards 3-PS2-1 expects students to predict the motion of an object with balanced or unbalanced forces and use this on the state assessment.

questions.prompt: When forces are not equal and a stronger push or pull changes an object's motion.
questions.explanation: Unbalanced forces happen when two opposing forces are not equal in size, so the object speeds up, slows down, stops, or changes direction. When you push a stationary skateboard, your push beats friction and the board starts moving. A falling apple has more gravity pulling down than air pushing up, so it accelerates. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing Grade 3 NGSS Standard 3-PS2-1) expects students to plan investigations to provide evidence of the effects of balanced and unbalanced forces on the motion of an object.

questions.prompt: When an object keeps moving at the same speed in a straight line because no unbalanced force acts on it.
questions.explanation: Newton's First Law (law of inertia) says an object at rest stays at rest, and an object in motion keeps moving in a straight line at constant speed, unless an unbalanced force acts on it. A puck on frictionless ice slides forever; a Long Island Rail Road train coasts only because the engine fights friction. Galileo proposed inertia around 1638, and Newton formalized it in 1687. NYS Grade 5 reviewing 3-PS2-1 builds toward inertia by having students model balanced forces producing no change in motion.

questions.prompt: The force that pushes back when you push on a wall, equal in size and opposite in direction.
questions.explanation: Newton's Third Law of Motion states that for every action force there is an equal and opposite reaction force. When you push a wall, the wall pushes back with the same strength. When a rocket pushes gases down, the gases push the rocket up. Sir Isaac Newton published this law in 1687 in his Principia Mathematica. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 3-PS2-1) and Grade 7 (MS-PS2-1, -2) build force-pair thinking, with the third-law reaction force being one of the most challenging Grade 5 assessment items.

questions.prompt: A change in the position of an object over time.
questions.explanation: Motion is the change in an object's position relative to a reference point over time. A walking student, a rolling ball, and a spinning Earth are all in motion. Motion can be measured as distance and time, which lets us calculate speed. Aristotle was the first to write systematically about motion around 350 BCE, though Galileo and Newton corrected his ideas. NYS Grade 5 reviewing Grade 3 NGSS 3-PS2-1 and 3-PS2-2 expects students to describe and predict patterns of an object's motion under the effects of forces.

questions.prompt: How fast an object is moving, found by dividing distance by time.
questions.explanation: Speed is the rate of motion: distance traveled divided by time taken (miles per hour, meters per second). A car driving from Long Island to New York City at 50 mph covers 50 miles every hour. Galileo Galilei studied speed and acceleration using inclined ramps around 1604. NYS Grade 5 reviewing Grade 3 NGSS 3-PS2-2 expects students to make observations and measurements of motion to provide evidence that patterns can predict future motion, and speed-time data appears on the Grade 5 state assessment.

questions.prompt: When an object's speed gets faster or slower over time, like a rolling car speeding up downhill.
questions.explanation: Acceleration is the rate at which an object's speed or direction changes over time. A ball rolling downhill accelerates because gravity pulls it; a car braking decelerates (negative acceleration). Acceleration is measured in m/s² (meters per second squared). Galileo first measured acceleration with inclined ramps around 1604, and Newton tied acceleration to force in 1687 (F = ma). NYS Grade 5 reviewing Grade 3 NGSS 3-PS2-1 and -2 expects students to recognize patterns of changing motion (acceleration) on graphs and data tables.

questions.prompt: The force that slows down a sliding object as one surface rubs against another.
questions.explanation: Friction is a force that resists motion between two surfaces in contact. Rough surfaces produce more friction than smooth ones. Friction slows a bicycle, lets your shoes grip the floor, and warms your hands when you rub them together. Without friction, you could not walk. Leonardo da Vinci sketched the first friction studies in the 1490s, and Charles-Augustin de Coulomb established friction laws in 1781. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 3-PS2-1) expects students to model friction as a force affecting motion.

questions.prompt: The property of objects with mass that resists changes in motion, making them hard to start or stop.
questions.explanation: Inertia is the tendency of an object to keep doing what it is already doing: staying still if at rest, or moving in a straight line at constant speed if moving. Objects with more mass have more inertia and are harder to start or stop. A bowling ball is harder to push than a soccer ball. Sir Isaac Newton named inertia in 1687, building on Galileo's earlier work. NYS Grade 5 reviewing 3-PS2-1 expects students to predict motion based on forces, and inertia (and Newton's First Law) is a top extension idea.

questions.prompt: A simple machine like a seesaw, with a rigid bar resting on a support, used to lift loads.
questions.explanation: A lever is a simple machine consisting of a rigid bar that pivots around a fixed point called a fulcrum, used to lift heavy loads with less effort. There are three classes of levers: first class (seesaw, scissors), second class (wheelbarrow, bottle opener), and third class (broom, fishing rod). Archimedes described levers around 250 BCE, saying 'Give me a place to stand and I will move the Earth.' NYS Grade 5 forces-and-motion ideas build toward simple machines as practical applications of force ideas.

questions.prompt: A round simple machine with a rod through its center, used in carts and cars.
questions.explanation: A wheel and axle is a simple machine that has a round wheel attached to a thinner rod (the axle); turning one part causes the other to turn, reducing the friction of dragging heavy objects. Examples include cars, bicycles, doorknobs, and the Long Island Rail Road trains. The Mesopotamians invented the potter's wheel around 4000 BCE and applied the wheel-and-axle to chariots around 3200 BCE. NYS Grade 5 forces and motion content includes simple machines as practical applications of reducing the force needed to move objects.

questions.prompt: A simple machine with a slanted flat surface, like a ramp, used to slide objects up or down.
questions.explanation: An inclined plane is a flat, sloped surface used to move heavy objects to a higher level with less force than lifting straight up. Examples include ramps, stairs, and roads up a hill. The pyramids of Giza were built around 2500 BCE using inclined planes. Trading less force for more distance is the key trick of all simple machines, first analyzed quantitatively by Archimedes around 250 BCE. NYS Grade 5 forces-and-motion ideas build toward inclined-plane investigations as common Grade 5 classroom experiments.

questions.prompt: A simple machine that is a rope or chain over a grooved wheel, used to lift loads.
questions.explanation: A pulley is a simple machine that uses a grooved wheel with a rope or chain around it to lift loads by changing the direction of the pulling force or, with multiple pulleys (block and tackle), reducing the force needed. Flagpoles, window blinds, and construction cranes use pulleys. Archimedes used a compound pulley around 250 BCE to single-handedly haul a fully loaded ship across the beach. NYS Grade 5 forces-and-motion ideas build toward simple machines as everyday examples of force and motion.

questions.prompt: A simple machine made of an inclined plane wrapped around a rod, like a wood screw.
questions.explanation: A screw is a simple machine that is essentially an inclined plane wound around a rod. Examples include wood screws, jar lids, drill bits, and the Archimedes screw used to lift water (still used in some Long Island marinas). Archimedes invented the water-screw around 250 BCE in Egypt. NYS Grade 5 forces-and-motion ideas include simple machines as practical demonstrations of how forces can be redirected or reduced, and a Grade 5 student should be able to identify the six classic simple machines and how each one helps move objects.

questions.prompt: The energy an object has because it is moving, like a rolling soccer ball.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy an object has due to its motion, calculated as 1/2 × mass × velocity squared (KE = ½mv²). A speeding bullet, a wave hitting a Long Island beach, and a thrown baseball all have kinetic energy. The faster the object or the more massive it is, the more kinetic energy it has. Lord Kelvin established the modern kinetic-energy formula in 1849. NYS Grade 5 ideas of forces and motion connect to Grade 4 NGSS 4-PS3-1, where students relate the speed of an object to the amount of energy it has.

questions.prompt: Stored energy that an object has because of its position or shape, like a ball at the top of a hill.
questions.explanation: Potential energy is stored energy an object has because of its position, shape, or condition. A book on a high shelf has gravitational potential energy that turns to kinetic energy when it falls. A stretched rubber band has elastic potential energy. Scottish engineer William Rankine introduced the term potential energy in 1853. NYS Grade 5 forces-and-motion (reviewing Grade 4 NGSS 4-PS3-2 and -3) expects students to make observations to provide evidence that energy can be transferred from place to place by motion, sound, light, heat, and electric currents.

questions.prompt: The scientific idea that energy is never created or destroyed, just changed from one form to another.
questions.explanation: Conservation of energy is the law that energy can change form (kinetic, potential, heat, light, electrical, chemical) but the total amount stays the same. A roller coaster's potential energy turns into kinetic energy and a tiny amount of heat from friction, but the total never disappears. German physician Julius von Mayer first stated the law in 1842 and James Joule confirmed it experimentally in 1845. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 4-PS3-1 through -4) expects students to model energy transfers and to identify that energy is never created or destroyed.

questions.prompt: Energy stored in food, batteries, and fossil fuels that is released by chemical reactions.
questions.explanation: Chemical energy is the energy stored in the bonds of atoms and molecules, released or absorbed during chemical reactions. The food a Grade 5 student eats stores chemical energy that the body releases through respiration to power motion and growth. Batteries, gasoline, and wood all store chemical energy. Antoine Lavoisier explained chemical-energy release in burning and breathing in the 1780s. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 4-PS3-4) expects students to apply scientific ideas to design and test devices that convert energy from one form to another.
