questions.prompt: The energy from a hot object that travels to a cooler object, like a pan warming food.
questions.explanation: Heat is the transfer of thermal energy from hotter objects to cooler ones until they reach the same temperature. Heat moves three ways: conduction (through touching objects), convection (through fluids), and radiation (through space). When you cook on a Long Island stovetop, heat moves by conduction. James Prescott Joule proved heat is a form of energy in 1845. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 4-PS3-2 and -3) expects students to make observations and measurements of energy transfers, with heat transfer being a top assessment example.

questions.prompt: The change of state where liquid water becomes water vapor as it heats up.
choices: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.explanation: Evaporation is the change from liquid to gas that happens as faster-moving particles escape the surface of a liquid into the air. It powers the water cycle by lifting water from oceans into the atmosphere. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-PS1-1, 5-PS1-2, and 5-ESS2-1 connect evaporation as a particle-level change of state, a mass-conserving process, and a key step in Earth's water cycle. Evaporation appears regularly on the Grade 5 state elementary science assessment as both a chemistry and an Earth-systems question.

questions.prompt: The continuous movement of water between oceans, the air, and land that brings rain.
choices: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.explanation: The water cycle is the continuous movement of water through evaporation, condensation, precipitation, runoff, and infiltration, powered by sunlight and gravity. About 97% of Earth's water is salty ocean and only 3% is fresh. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-ESS2-1 and 5-ESS2-2 expect students to model how water moves through Earth's four spheres and to graph the percentages of fresh and salt water in different reservoirs, both top-frequency NYS Grade 5 elementary science assessment items.

questions.prompt: A mixture where one substance dissolves completely and evenly into another, like sugar water.
choices: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.explanation: A solution is a mixture where one substance (solute) dissolves evenly in another (solvent). Salt water, sugar water, and Long Island Sound water are solutions. Solutions cannot be separated by filtering. Swedish chemist Svante Arrhenius developed the modern theory of solutions in 1884. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-PS1-3 and 5-PS1-4 expect students to identify materials by properties and to test whether mixing creates new substances, with solutions being a top example of a mixture, not a chemical change.

questions.prompt: The single biggest source of energy for nearly every process in Earth's living systems.
choices: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.explanation: The Sun is the medium-sized star at the center of our solar system whose fusion of hydrogen into helium powers nearly every process on Earth: weather, the water cycle, ocean currents, and the chemical energy in food. Hans Bethe explained solar fusion in 1939. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-PS3-1 and 5-ESS1-1 expect students to argue that the Sun is the source of energy in animals' food and that it looks brighter than other stars because it is closer to Earth, two foundational Grade 5 ideas.

questions.prompt: The four classic clues that a chemical change has occurred, like bubbles or a color change.
choices: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.correctText: Chemical change clues (gas, color change, temperature change, light)
questions.explanation: The four classic clues of a chemical change are: a new gas (bubbles), a color change (rusting), a temperature change (hot or cold), and light or odor. These signs mean a new substance with new properties has formed and the change is hard to reverse. Antoine Lavoisier classified chemical reactions in the 1780s. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-4 directly asks students to conduct an investigation to determine whether mixing substances forms a new substance, the most heavily tested chemistry idea on the Grade 5 NYS elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: Green organisms that turn sunlight, water, and carbon dioxide into food.
questions.explanation: Producers (plants, algae, some bacteria) form the base of every food chain by capturing sunlight through photosynthesis and storing it as glucose. Jan Ingenhousz showed in 1779 that green plants in light release oxygen. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-LS1-1 and 5-LS2-1 expect students to argue that plants get materials chiefly from air and water, and to model how matter and energy move from producers to consumers and decomposers in an ecosystem. This is the most-tested ecosystem item on the NYS Grade 5 elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The tiny units of matter, too small to see, that make up all things.
questions.explanation: Particles (atoms and molecules) are the tiny units that make up all matter, too small to see with the unaided eye. Greek philosopher Democritus first proposed atoms in 400 BCE, and John Dalton confirmed atoms scientifically in 1808. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-1 expects students to develop a model to describe that matter is made of particles too small to be seen, the foundation concept of the entire Grade 5 matter unit and a top-frequency item on the state Grade 5 elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: Bacteria and fungi that break down dead things and return nutrients to the soil.
questions.explanation: Decomposers like soil bacteria and mushrooms break down dead organisms and waste, recycling nitrogen, carbon, and phosphorus into the soil for new producers. Antonie van Leeuwenhoek first saw bacteria in his 1670s microscopes. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS2-1 directly expects students to develop a model that describes the movement of matter among plants, animals, decomposers, and the environment, with decomposers being the recycling step that distinguishes matter cycling from one-way energy flow on the state Grade 5 science test.

questions.prompt: The law that says total mass stays the same during physical or chemical changes.
questions.explanation: Conservation of mass is the scientific law that the total amount of matter stays the same before and after physical or chemical changes. French chemist Antoine Lavoisier proved this in 1789 with sealed-flask burning experiments. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS1-2 directly asks students to measure and graph quantities to provide evidence that regardless of the type of change, the total weight of matter is conserved, a core matter concept on the Grade 5 state elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The diagram that shows many connected food chains linking producers, consumers, and decomposers.
questions.explanation: A food web is the standard NYS Grade 5 model that shows how matter cycles and energy flows through producers, consumers, and decomposers. Energy moves one way from the Sun to producers to consumers, while matter is recycled by decomposers. Charles Elton built the modern food-web concept in 1927. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-LS2-1 directly asks students to develop a food-web model, which is one of the top-frequency open-response items on the Grade 5 state elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: Earth's spin once every 24 hours that causes day and night.
questions.explanation: Rotation is the daily 24-hour spin of Earth on its axis, producing day and night. Léon Foucault's 1851 pendulum experiment in Paris proved Earth rotates. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to graph patterns of daily and seasonal changes in shadow length and Sun position, all explained by rotation and revolution. Grade 5 students must distinguish rotation (daily, day/night) from revolution (yearly, seasons), and this distinction is a frequent state Grade 5 assessment trap.

questions.prompt: Earth's yearly orbit around the Sun, taking about 365 days.
questions.explanation: Revolution is Earth's yearly 365.25-day trip around the Sun, which (combined with Earth's 23.5° axis tilt) produces the four seasons. Nicolaus Copernicus established Earth's revolution around the Sun in 1543, replacing the Earth-centered model. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to graph seasonal patterns of sunrise and sunset positions and length of day, all explained by Earth's tilted revolution. Distinguishing revolution from rotation is a top-frequency Grade 5 assessment item.

questions.prompt: The reason the Sun looks much brighter than the next-closest star.
questions.explanation: The Sun looks brighter than all other stars because it is only 93 million miles away, while the next-closest star (Proxima Centauri) is more than 25 trillion miles away. Friedrich Bessel made the first reliable star-distance measurement in 1838. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 directly asks students to support an argument that the apparent brightness of the Sun and stars is due to their relative distances from Earth, one of the most frequently tested concepts on the Grade 5 state elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The repeating changing shapes of the Moon during its monthly orbit around Earth.
questions.explanation: Moon phases are the eight repeating shapes (new, crescent, quarter, gibbous, full) the Moon shows during its 29.5-day orbit around Earth. Half of the Moon is always lit by the Sun, but we see different fractions of that lit half. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-2 expects students to represent data in graphical displays to reveal patterns in the appearance of the Moon, which is a frequent Grade 5 state elementary science assessment item, often paired with a Moon-phase calendar.

questions.prompt: Earth, Mars, Mercury, Venus, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune are these objects held by the Sun's gravity.
questions.explanation: Planets are the eight largest bodies orbiting the Sun: Mercury, Venus, Earth, Mars, Jupiter, Saturn, Uranus, and Neptune. They are held in orbit by the Sun's gravity, with 99.86% of the solar system's mass. Nicolaus Copernicus established the Sun-centered model in 1543, and Galileo's 1610 telescope observations confirmed it. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-ESS1-1 builds toward understanding the solar system as objects held by the Sun's gravity, and 5-PS2-1 connects this to Earth's downward gravity on all objects.

questions.prompt: The pulling force that always points toward Earth's center, making things fall down.
questions.explanation: Gravity is the invisible attractive force every object with mass exerts on every other object. On Earth, gravity always pulls toward the planet's center at 9.8 m/s², which we experience as 'down.' Sir Isaac Newton formulated the law of universal gravitation in 1687. NYS Grade 5 Standard 5-PS2-1 directly asks students to support an argument that the gravitational force exerted by Earth on objects is directed down toward the planet's center, the highest-frequency Grade 5 physical-science assessment item.

questions.prompt: When two opposite forces on an object are equal in size, so the object's motion does not change.
questions.explanation: Balanced forces are equal-strength forces in opposite directions, canceling each other so an object's motion does not change. A book resting on a Long Island classroom desk has balanced forces: gravity pulls down, the desk pushes up. Newton's First Law (1687) explains balanced forces. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing Grade 3 NGSS 3-PS2-1) expects students to plan investigations of how balanced versus unbalanced forces affect motion, a recurring Grade 5 state elementary science assessment item.

questions.prompt: The force that resists motion between two surfaces touching each other, like a sliding book.
questions.explanation: Friction is a force that opposes the relative motion of two surfaces in contact. Friction lets shoes grip the ground, slows a sliding hockey puck, and warms your hands when rubbed together. Leonardo da Vinci sketched friction studies in the 1490s, and Charles-Augustin de Coulomb established friction laws in 1781. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 3-PS2-1) expects students to model how forces, including friction, change an object's motion, and friction often appears on the Grade 5 state elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: Data shown as a picture, like a bar graph or line graph, to find patterns.
questions.explanation: A graph is a visual display of data that helps scientists and students see patterns: bar graphs compare amounts, line graphs show change over time, and pie charts show parts of a whole. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-PS1-2, 5-ESS2-2, and 5-ESS1-2 specifically ask students to represent data in graphical displays. Florence Nightingale developed the modern statistical graph in 1858. Constructing and interpreting graphs is a top-frequency NYS Grade 5 elementary science assessment skill.

questions.prompt: The scientific law that says energy is never created or destroyed, only changed in form.
questions.explanation: Conservation of energy is the law that energy can change form (kinetic, potential, heat, light, electrical, chemical) but the total amount stays the same. A roller coaster's potential energy turns into kinetic energy and heat, but the total never changes. German physician Julius von Mayer first stated the law in 1842 and James Joule confirmed it in 1845. NYS Grade 5 (reviewing 4-PS3-1 through -4) expects students to model and identify energy transfers across systems.

questions.prompt: Using your eyes, ears, or instruments to gather information about the natural world.
questions.explanation: Observation is the science practice of gathering information about the natural world using the senses or instruments (rulers, thermometers, balances). Observations can be qualitative (color, smell) or quantitative (mass, length). NYS Grade 5 Science Learning Standards build on NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 3 (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations), where students make and record observations to gather evidence. This is the foundational skill of every science classroom and a building block for higher-order Grade 5 assessment tasks.

questions.prompt: A possible explanation for an observation that can be tested through experiments.
questions.explanation: A hypothesis is a possible, testable explanation for an observation, often written as 'If [I do X], then [Y will happen].' Francis Bacon laid the foundation for hypothesis-driven science in 1620. NYS Grade 5 Science aligns with NGSS Science and Engineering Practices 1, 3, and 4 (Asking Questions, Planning Investigations, Analyzing Data) which expect students to form hypotheses and design tests, especially in the experimental open-response items on the state Grade 5 elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: An organized way to test a hypothesis by changing one factor while keeping others the same.
questions.explanation: A controlled experiment is a test where scientists change only one variable (the independent variable) and measure its effect (the dependent variable) while keeping all other factors the same (controlled variables). English scientist Francis Bacon laid the foundation for controlled experiments in 1620. NYS Grade 5 Science aligns with NGSS Science and Engineering Practice 3 (Planning and Carrying Out Investigations), which is the most heavily emphasized investigation-design skill on the state Grade 5 elementary science assessment.

questions.prompt: The energy a moving object has, like a rolling ball or a flying baseball.
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy is the energy of motion. It depends on mass and speed: KE = ½mv². A bowling ball has more kinetic energy than a tennis ball at the same speed. Lord Kelvin established the modern kinetic-energy formula in 1849. NYS Grade 5 reviewing Grade 4 NGSS 4-PS3-1 expects students to use evidence to relate the speed of an object to the energy it has, with kinetic energy being a top assessment example on the NYS Grade 5 elementary science test.

questions.prompt: Drawings, diagrams, or computer simulations scientists use to explain how a system works.
questions.explanation: A model is a representation (drawing, diagram, computer simulation, or physical object) used to explain or predict how a system works. NYS Grade 5 Standards 5-PS1-1, 5-LS2-1, 5-ESS2-1, and 5-PS3-1 all directly ask students to develop models of particles, food webs, Earth systems, and energy flow. American educator Joseph Schwab promoted models in 1960s science teaching. Building and using models is one of the most heavily emphasized NYS Grade 5 elementary science assessment skills, and connects to NGSS Practice 2.
